examples.title: Transmission Mode Simulation
examples.description: Simulates Simplex, Half-Duplex, and Full-Duplex transmission modes using Python classes
examples.code:
class TransmissionMode:
    """Base class for transmission modes"""
    def send(self, message, sender, receiver):
        raise NotImplementedError


class Simplex(TransmissionMode):
    """Simplex: Data flows in one direction only"""
    def send(self, message, sender, receiver):
        print(f"[{sender} -> {receiver}] SIMPLEX: '{message}'")
        print("  Status: Transmission successful (one-way only)")
        return True


class HalfDuplex(TransmissionMode):
    """Half-Duplex: Data flows in both directions but not simultaneously"""
    def __init__(self):
        self.channel_busy = False
    
    def send(self, message, sender, receiver):
        if self.channel_busy:
            print(f"  Status: Channel busy, waiting...")
            self.channel_busy = False
        
        print(f"[{sender} -> {receiver}] HALF-DUPLEX: '{message}'")
        self.channel_busy = True
        print("  Status: Transmission complete, channel now available")
        self.channel_busy = False
        return True


class FullDuplex(TransmissionMode):
    """Full-Duplex: Data flows in both directions simultaneously"""
    def send(self, message, sender, receiver):
        print(f"[{sender} <-> {receiver}] FULL-DUPLEX: '{message}'")
        print("  Status: Bidirectional transmission (simultaneous)")
        return True


# Demonstration
if __name__ == "__main__":
    print("=" * 60)
    print("TRANSMISSION MODES DEMONSTRATION")
    print("=" * 60)
    
    # Simplex Mode
    print("\n1. SIMPLEX MODE (Television/Radio)")
    print("-" * 40)
    simplex = Simplex()
    simplex.send("Broadcast signal", "TV Station", "Television")
    
    # Half-Duplex Mode
    print("\n2. HALF-DUPLEX MODE (Walkie-Talkie)")
    print("-" * 40)
    half_duplex = HalfDuplex()
    half_duplex.send("Hello", "Device A", "Device B")
    half_duplex.send("Response", "Device B", "Device A")
    
    # Full-Duplex Mode
    print("\n3. FULL-DUPLEX MODE (Telephone)")
    print("-" * 40)
    full_duplex = FullDuplex()
    full_duplex.send("Hello", "Caller", "Receiver")
    full_duplex.send("Hi there", "Receiver", "Caller")
examples.explanation: This code demonstrates three transmission modes: (1) Simplex - data flows in one direction only (like TV broadcast), (2) Half-Duplex - data can flow both directions but not simultaneously (like walkie-talkie), (3) Full-Duplex - data flows both directions simultaneously (like telephone). Each class implements a send() method that simulates how data transmission works in each mode.

examples.title: Network Switch with Transmission Modes
examples.description: Simulates a network switch that handles frames and operates in different duplex modes
examples.code:
from collections import deque
import time

class NetworkDevice:
    """Base class for network devices"""
    def __init__(self, name):
        self.name = name
        self.mac_address_table = {}
    
    def learn_mac(self, port, mac_address):
        """Learn MAC address and associate with port"""
        self.mac_address_table[mac_address] = port
        print(f"  [SWITCH] MAC {mac_address} learned on port {port}")


class Switch(NetworkDevice):
    """Network Switch - operates at Layer 2 (Data Link Layer)"""
    def __init__(self, name, duplex_mode="full"):
        super().__init__(name)
        self.duplex_mode = duplex_mode
        self.ports = {
            1: {"device": None, "mac": None},
            2: {"device": None, "mac": None},
            3: {"device": None, "mac": None},
            4: {"device": None, "mac": None}
        }
        self.frame_buffer = deque()
    
    def connect_device(self, port, device_name, mac_address):
        """Connect a device to a switch port"""
        if port in self.ports:
            self.ports[port]["device"] = device_name
            self.ports[port]["mac"] = mac_address
            self.learn_mac(port, mac_address)
            print(f"[SWITCH] {device_name} connected to port {port}")
    
    def receive_frame(self, src_mac, dest_mac, data, src_port):
        """Receive and process a frame"""
        print(f"\n[SWITCH] Frame received from {src_mac} at port {src_port}")
        
        # Learn source MAC
        self.learn_mac(src_port, src_mac)
        
        # Look up destination MAC
        dest_port = self.mac_address_table.get(dest_mac)
        
        if dest_port:
            # Known destination - forward to specific port
            print(f"[SWITCH] Destination known - forwarding to port {dest_port}")
            self._forward_frame(src_port, dest_port, src_mac, dest_mac, data)
        else:
            # Unknown destination - flood (except source port)
            print(f"[SWITCH] Unknown destination - flooding all ports except {src_port}")
            self._flood_frame(src_port, src_mac, dest_mac, data)
    
    def _forward_frame(self, src_port, dest_port, src_mac, dest_mac, data):
        """Forward frame to specific port"""
        if self.duplex_mode == "full":
            print(f"  [{self.name}] FULL-DUPLEX: Frame forwarded {src_port} -> {dest_port}")
        else:
            print(f"  [{self.name}] HALF-DUPLEX: Frame forwarded {src_port} -> {dest_port}")
        print(f"  Payload: '{data}'")
    
    def _flood_frame(self, exclude_port, src_mac, dest_mac, data):
        """Flood frame to all ports except source"""
        for port in self.ports:
            if port != exclude_port and self.ports[port]["device"]:
                print(f"  [{self.name}] Flooding to port {port}")


# Demonstration
if __name__ == "__main__":
    print("=" * 60)
    print("NETWORK SWITCH SIMULATION")
    print("=" * 60)
    
    # Create switch with Full-Duplex mode
    switch = Switch("Switch-1", duplex_mode="full")
    
    # Connect devices
    print("\n--- Connecting Devices ---")
    switch.connect_device(1, "PC-A", "AA:BB:CC:DD:01")
    switch.connect_device(2, "PC-B", "AA:BB:CC:DD:02")
    switch.connect_device(3, "PC-C", "AA:BB:CC:DD:03")
    
    # Frame transmission
    print("\n--- Frame Transmission ---")
    
    # PC-A sends to PC-B (known destination)
    switch.receive_frame(
        src_mac="AA:BB:CC:DD:01",
        dest_mac="AA:BB:CC:DD:02",
        data="Hello PC-B",
        src_port=1
    )
    
    # PC-A sends to PC-D (unknown destination)
    switch.receive_frame(
        src_mac="AA:BB:CC:DD:01",
        dest_mac="AA:BB:CC:DD:04",
        data="Hello unknown device",
        src_port=1
    )
    
    # PC-B responds to PC-A
    switch.receive_frame(
        src_mac="AA:BB:CC:DD:02",
        dest_mac="AA:BB:CC:DD:01",
        data="Hi PC-A, message received",
        src_port=2
    )
examples.explanation: This code simulates a network switch (Layer 2 device) that learns MAC addresses and forwards frames. It demonstrates: (1) MAC address learning - switch builds a table mapping MAC addresses to ports, (2) Frame forwarding - when destination is known, frame is sent to specific port, (3) Frame flooding - when destination is unknown, frame is flooded to all ports except source, (4) Full-duplex operation - simultaneous bidirectional communication.
examples.timeComplexity: O(1) for MAC lookup, O(n) for flooding
examples.spaceComplexity: O(n) where n is number of connected devices

examples.title: Network Topology with Multiple Devices
examples.description: Simulates a complete network topology with routers, switches, and end devices demonstrating routing and transmission modes
examples.code:
import random
from enum import Enum

class DeviceType(Enum):
    ROUTER = "Router"
    SWITCH = "Switch"
    END_DEVICE = "End Device"


class NetworkDevice:
    """Generic network device"""
    def __init__(self, name, device_type, ip_address=None):
        self.name = name
        self.device_type = device_type
        self.ip_address = ip_address
        self.connected_devices = []
    
    def connect(self, device):
        """Connect to another device"""
        self.connected_devices.append(device)
        device.connected_devices.append(self)
        print(f"  {self.name} <--> {device.name} connected")
    
    def send_packet(self, dest_ip, data):
        """Send packet (default behavior)"""
        print(f"{self.name}: Sending '{data}' to {dest_ip}")


class Router(NetworkDevice):
    """Router - operates at Layer 3 (Network Layer)"""
    def __init__(self, name, ip_address):
        super().__init__(name, DeviceType.ROUTER, ip_address)
        self.routing_table = {}
    
    def add_route(self, network, next_hop):
        """Add entry to routing table"""
        self.routing_table[network] = next_hop
    
    def route_packet(self, dest_ip):
        """Determine next hop for packet"""
        # Simple longest prefix match simulation
        for network in self.routing_table:
            if dest_ip.startswith(network):
                return self.routing_table[network]
        return None


class EndDevice(NetworkDevice):
    """End device (PC, Server, etc.)"""
    def __init__(self, name, ip_address, transmission_mode="full-duplex"):
        super().__init__(name, DeviceType.END_DEVICE, ip_address)
        self.transmission_mode = transmission_mode
        self.received_packets = []
    
    def send_packet(self, dest_ip, data, network):
        """Send packet through network"""
        print(f"\n[{self.name} ({self.transmission_mode})] Sending to {dest_ip}")
        print(f"  Data: {data}")
        
        # Find connected switch/router
        for device in self.connected_devices:
            if device.device_type in [DeviceType.SWITCH, DeviceType.ROUTER]:
                device.forward_packet(self.ip_address, dest_ip, data)
                break
    
    def receive_packet(self, data, sender):
        """Receive packet"""
        self.received_packets.append(data)
        print(f"  >>> {self.name} received: '{data}' from {sender}")


class Switch(NetworkDevice):
    """Network Switch"""
    def __init__(self, name):
        super().__init__(name, DeviceType.SWITCH)
        self.mac_table = {}
    
    def forward_packet(self, src_ip, dest_ip, data):
        """Forward packet based on IP (simplified)"""
        print(f"  [SWITCH {self.name}] Forwarding packet...")
        
        # Forward to all connected devices except source
        for device in self.connected_devices:
            if device.ip_address != src_ip:
                if hasattr(device, 'receive_packet'):
                    device.receive_packet(data, src_ip)


# Demonstration
if __name__ == "__main__":
    print("=" * 70)
    print("NETWORK TOPOLOGY SIMULATION")
    print("=" * 70)
    
    # Create network devices
    router = Router("Router-1", "192.168.1.1")
    router.add_route("192.168.2.", "192.168.2.1")
    router.add_route("192.168.3.", "192.168.3.1")
    
    switch1 = Switch("Switch-A")
    switch2 = Switch("Switch-B")
    
    pc1 = EndDevice("PC-1", "192.168.1.10", "full-duplex")
    pc2 = EndDevice("PC-2", "192.168.2.10", "full-duplex")
    pc3 = EndDevice("PC-3", "192.168.3.10", "half-duplex")
    server = EndDevice("Server-1", "192.168.1.100", "full-duplex")
    
    # Create topology
    print("\n--- Building Network Topology ---")
    print("Network A: 192.168.1.0/24")
    print("Network B: 192.168.2.0/24")
    print("Network C: 192.168.3.0/24")
    
    router.connect(switch1)
    router.connect(switch2)
    switch1.connect(pc1)
    switch1.connect(server)
    switch2.connect(pc2)
    switch2.connect(pc3)
    
    # Test communication within same network (full-duplex)
    print("\n" + "=" * 70)
    print("TEST 1: Full-Duplex Communication (PC-1 <-> Server)")
    print("=" * 70)
    pc1.send_packet("192.168.1.100", "GET /index.html HTTP/1.1", "192.168.1.0")
    server.receive_packet("HTTP 200 OK - Here is your page", "192.168.1.10")
    
    # Test communication across router
    print("\n" + "=" * 70)
    print("TEST 2: Cross-Network Communication (PC-1 -> PC-2)")
    print("=" * 70)
    pc1.send_packet("192.168.2.10", "Ping request", "192.168.1.0")
    next_hop = router.route_packet("192.168.2.10")
    print(f"  [ROUTER] Next hop for 192.168.2.10: {next_hop}")
    
    # Test half-duplex device
    print("\n" + "=" * 70)
    print("TEST 3: Half-Duplex Device (PC-3)")
    print("=" * 70)
    print(f"  [PC-3] Operating in {pc3.transmission_mode} mode")
    print("  Note: Can only send OR receive at one time, not both")
    pc3.send_packet("192.168.3.1", "Router ping", "192.168.3.0")
examples.explanation: This advanced example simulates a complete network topology with: (1) Routers - Layer 3 devices that route packets between networks using routing tables, (2) Switches - Layer 2 devices that forward frames within a network, (3) End Devices - computers/servers with different transmission modes (full-duplex, half-duplex), (4) Network connectivity - devices connected in a realistic topology, (5) Packet forwarding - demonstrates how data flows through multiple network devices from source to destination.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) for routing and forwarding where n is network size
examples.spaceComplexity: O(n) for storing routing tables and network connections
